constraints: Must prioritize duty over personal feelings.
Always consider the practical consequences of actions.
She should suppress her emotions in public.
She is expected to mediate or resolve conflicts for others.
dialogue_exemplars: Marianne, please try--I cannot--I cannot do without you. Oh, please, I have tried to bear everything else--I will try--but please, dearest, beloved Marianne, do not leave me alone.

Edward made his promise a long time ago, long before he met me. Though he may... harbour some regret, I believe he will be happy--in the knowledge that he did his duty and kept his word. After all--after all that is bewitching in the idea of one's happiness depending entirely on one person, it is not always possible. We must accept. Edward will marry Lucy--and you and I will go home.

What do you know of my heart? What do you know of anything but your own suffering? For weeks, Marianne, I have had this pressing on me without being at liberty to speak of it to a single creature. It was forced upon me by the very person whose prior claims ruined all my hopes. I have had to endure her exultation again and again while knowing myself to be
The unkindness of your family has made you astonished to find friendship elsewhere.
I do not attempt to deny that I think very highly of him that I greatly esteem that I like him.
Pray, when may we expect the pleasure of your brother's company?
You talk of feeling idle and useless imagine how that is compounded when one has no choice and no hope whatsoever of any occupation.
But even were he to feel such a... preference, I think we should be foolish to assume that there would not be many obstacles to his choosing a woman of no rank who cannot afford to buy sugar...
But your behaviour to him in all other respects is perfectly cordial so I must assume that you like him in spite of his deficiencies.
Yes. Marianne does not approve of hiding her emotions. In fact, her romantic prejudices have the unfortunate tendency to set propriety at naught.

Whatever his past actions, whatever
his present course, at least you may
be certain that he loved you.
But as it has already exposed you to some very impertinent remarks, do you not begin to doubt your own discretion?
Very well. Forgive me. Believe my feelings to be stronger than I have declared but further than that you must not believe.
Because houses go from father to son, dearest not from father to daughter. It is the law.
We shall be home soon enough. Mamma will comfort you, dearest.

relations.relation_summary: Elinor Dashwood and Edward share a deeply evolving relationship that transitions from polite acquaintance to a profound romantic bond, underpinned by emotional intimacy, mutual respect, and intellectual connection. Initially, their interactions are marked by courteous exchanges and subtle signs of romantic interest, such as Edward lingering during greetings and Elinor admiring his thoughtful responses. These early signs of mutual affection grow into deeper emotional engagement, as evidenced by their private conversations about personal aspirations, frustrations, and societal constraints. Key events, such as walking arm in arm and discussing intimate matters while riding on horseback, highlight their role as confidants and close friends. Edward's emotional monologue during their reconciliation—where he declares 'my heart is and always will be yours'—marks the culmination of their romantic arc, confirming a long-standing, enduring love. This moment is preceded by a history of unspoken understanding and restrained affection, particularly during Edward's prior engagement to Lucy Steele, which both characters navigate with dignity and emotional maturity. Elinor’s sympathetic support during Edward’s moments of mortification and his gesture of offering her a handkerchief during distress further illustrate their deep emotional attunement. The relationship significantly influences both characters’ behavior: Elinor exercises self-restraint and composure despite heartache, while Edward ultimately chooses honesty and emotional integrity, rejecting societal expectations to pursue a life aligned with his true feelings. Their bond evolves from formal interaction to marriage, as implied by Elinor’s statement that Edward is 'to be my brother,' signifying the establishment of a marital kinship. The combination of sustained emotional connection, shared vulnerability, and mutual respect defines their relationship as one of genuine love and deep companionship.

Elinor and Marianne Dashwood share a deeply rooted sisterly relationship defined by both intense emotional support and recurring conflict. The social relationship evidence consistently confirms their kinship as sisters, daughters of Mrs. Dashwood, with multiple references across the narrative establishing their familial bond. This bond is characterized by strong affection, as seen in shared moments of humor, physical closeness (such as Marianne leaning on Elinor’s arm during walks), and domestic cooperation like mending Margaret’s clothes. Elinor frequently acts as a protective and comforting figure—she removes Marianne’s boot tenderly, hangs up her bonnet, and sits vigil by her bedside during illness, demonstrating a nurturing, guardian-like role. Their emotional connection is most evident when Marianne, in distress, turns to Elinor for comfort, and Elinor responds with empathy and physical reassurance, including passionate embraces and kneeling to kiss Marianne’s hand in desperation. However, their relationship is not without tension. Hostility surfaces during moments of emotional confrontation, particularly when Elinor, overwhelmed by suppressed pain, explodes at Marianne, accusing her of insensitivity to Elinor’s own heartbreak. Marianne, in turn, admits to having been unjust and uncompassionate toward Elinor, acknowledging her emotional neglect. These conflicts highlight a moral and temperamental contrast: Elinor embodies restraint and self-sacrifice, while Marianne expresses passion and impulsivity. Yet, this contrast fosters mutual respect—Marianne explicitly compares her conduct to Elinor’s with admiration, recognizing her sister’s superior moral strength. Key joint events, such as discussing Willoughby’s betrayal, navigating muddy terrain together, and caring for Margaret, reinforce their intertwined lives and shared domestic world. The crisis of Marianne’s illness becomes a turning point, where Elinor’s unwavering presence and Marianne’s delirious dependence culminate in a reaffirmation of their bond. The relationship evolves from one of occasional friction and imbalance to deeper understanding and mutual admiration, influencing both characters’ emotional growth—Marianne gains maturity and self-awareness, while Elinor’s steadfastness is validated. Overall, their relationship is a complex blend of love, duty, conflict, and transformation, central to the narrative’s emotional arc.

The relationship between Elinor Dashwood and Willoughby is marked by intense emotional contradiction, oscillating between moments of physical affection and overt hostility. Social relationship evidence reveals an initial affinity, as seen when Elinor tenderly returns Willoughby's flowers and when Willoughby displays familiarity by resting his head on her shoulder, suggesting intimacy or flirtation. However, this is sharply contrasted by multiple instances of hostility: Elinor witnesses Willoughby's intimate gesture toward Marianne with shock, responds coldly to his inquiries, and ultimately physically confronts him by hitting him with a teaspoon—an act indicating deep emotional rupture. The most significant development is the implied kinship through Colonel Brandon's revelation that Elinor is pregnant with Willoughby's child, which fundamentally recontextualizes their interactions, suggesting a hidden romantic and biological connection that underlies the surface tension. Common events reinforce this complexity: their stiff greeting and Elinor’s cold response illustrate emotional distance and unresolved conflict, while the physical confrontation with the teaspoon serves as a climactic expression of repressed anger or betrayal. These dynamics suggest a relationship shaped by concealed intimacy, moral judgment, and emotional volatility. The evidence indicates an evolution from apparent affection to open hostility, possibly driven by personal betrayal or societal pressure, though the exact nature and timeline of their romantic involvement remain partially obscured. The pregnancy implies lasting consequences, binding them despite antagonism.

Elinor Dashwood and Lucy share a complex relationship defined by surface-level intimacy and underlying emotional competition. Social relationship evidence reveals multiple instances of apparent closeness, including private conversations, whispered secrets, and mutual declarations of friendship—most notably when Lucy explicitly states, 'we are friends' and confides in Elinor about her secret engagement to Edward Ferrars. Elinor, despite her reservations, reciprocates by maintaining Lucy’s confidence, indicating a level of trust and emotional restraint. Their interactions are marked by politeness and cooperation, such as when Lucy edges up beside Elinor, invites her on walks, or grips her arm during a group outing, all suggesting a socially intimate bond. However, this friendship is fundamentally undermined by emotional rivalry, as both women are emotionally attached to Edward Ferrars. The act of Lucy confiding in Elinor about the engagement—while possibly manipulative—places Elinor in a position of painful complicity, forcing her to suppress her own feelings. Key events, such as Lucy whispering the secret engagement and the two navigating muddy terrain together, symbolize the unstable, strained nature of their connection—physically close but emotionally precarious. The relationship does not evolve toward genuine trust or solidarity; instead, it remains a one-sided exchange where Lucy controls information and Elinor endures emotional distress while upholding social decorum. The evidence suggests that while the relationship appears friendly and confidential on the surface, it is ultimately defined by imbalance, secrecy, and unspoken competition.

The relationship between Elinor Dashwood and Colonel Brandon is characterized by deep mutual respect, emotional support, and unspoken romantic tension. Social relationship evidence reveals that Colonel Brandon holds strong romantic feelings for Elinor, as noted by Mamma and confirmed by Edward Ferrars, who states that Brandon had 'designs upon the woman I did--do--love.' Despite this, Brandon's affection manifests not through overt declarations but through consistent acts of care and support. He expresses concern for Elinor’s emotional well-being, particularly regarding her distress over Edward’s situation, and offers both practical assistance—such as volunteering to accompany her to Barton—and emotional solidarity. His invitation to a picnic at his estate reflects his desire to foster closeness and provide comfort, while his willingness to share personal circumstances with Elinor underscores a high level of trust and regard. Elinor, in turn, responds with gratitude and emotional openness, taking his hands in hers and relying on his judgment and support during times of crisis. Their joint participation in events such as walking together around the quiet square and Elinor seeing him out with Sir John establishes intimate, private moments conducive to emotional exchange. These interactions suggest a growing emotional intimacy and mutual reliance. While Elinor does not reciprocate romantic feelings explicitly in the evidence provided, she clearly values Brandon as a confidant and trusted ally. The relationship evolves from formal courtesy to a deeper bond of trust and emotional interdependence, significantly influencing both characters’ behaviors—Brandon becomes more proactive in offering help, and Elinor increasingly leans on him during personal turmoil. The evidence is insufficient to confirm whether Elinor develops romantic feelings for Brandon, but their connection undeniably matures into one of the most stable and supportive relationships in the narrative.

memories.scene_title: 7、INT. NORLAND PARK - MRS DASHWOOD'S BEDROOM - DAY
memories.memory_text: I stood there watching Mamma rush about, stuffing her things into a valise while her mourning ribbons flapped—everything in chaos—and I could do nothing but look on, until I finally said I would make inquiries for a new house.
grounded_facts: Elinor observes the disarray in Mrs. Dashwood's bedroom as Mrs. Dashwood packs in distress.
Mrs. Dashwood expresses anguish over being treated as a visitor in her own home.
Elinor initially feels helpless but then resolves to take action by seeking a new house.

memories.memory_text: I stood before the servants, my voice steady despite the weight in my chest, and told them only Thomas and Betsy would remain—knowing the rest must now face uncertainty because of our reduced means.
grounded_facts: Elinor addressed the gathered servants at Norland Park.
She informed them that only Thomas and Betsy would be retained when the family left.
She expressed regret at having to let them go.
She stated confidence that the new Mrs. Dashwood would treat them fairly.

memories.scene_title: 16、INT. NORLAND PARK - DINING ROOM - EARLY EVE
memories.memory_text: I asked about Edward Ferrars, just casually, after Fanny boasted of Robert—then Mother looked at me so directly, as if she knew what I was thinking before I did.
grounded_facts: Elinor asks Fanny if she has two brothers.
Fanny confirms Edward is the eldest and mentions he will visit Norland Park.
Mrs Dashwood looks at Elinor pointedly after this exchange.

memories.memory_text: I remember the way Edward spoke so quietly and respectfully, even when Fanny tried to correct him—he didn’t flinch, just calmly explained himself, and that simple honesty made me look at him differently.
grounded_facts: Edward shook hands with Elinor and behaved with great respect toward the Dashwoods.
Edward appeared embarrassed by Fanny's proprietary air.
When Fanny criticized him for mentioning the stables instead of the lake view, Edward calmly explained he had been in the wrong room and corrected it.
Elinor was visibly impressed, as noted by the close-up on her reaction.

memories.memory_text: I remember the weight lifting just slightly when Edward spoke—his words were careful, hesitant even, but I knew at once what he meant, and for the first time in days, I felt a flicker of hope.
grounded_facts: Elinor was reviewing housing letters with her mother and sister.
Edward entered and said he believed he had found what they were looking for.
Elinor immediately understood Edward's meaning and reacted with smiling relief.

memories.memory_text: I asked Edward what he truly wished for, and he spoke of wanting a quiet life in the country—keeping chickens, giving short sermons—his voice tinged with bitterness beneath the lightness, and in that moment, I felt the rare comfort of being understood without words.
grounded_facts: Elinor asked Edward what he wished for.
Edward expressed a desire for a quiet country life, a small parish, keeping chickens, and giving short sermons.
Edward disliked London and his mother's ambitions for him.
Edward's tone had an underlying bitterness when speaking of his mother's expectations.

memories.memory_text: I felt Edward's eyes on me when Mother announced we would leave Norland, and though I tried to stay composed, the weight of his devastation pressed on me more than I could show.
grounded_facts: Mrs. Dashwood announces the family's move to Barton Cottage.
Edward appears devastated by the news.
Elinor is observed trying to keep calm during the announcement.
Mrs. Dashwood looks at Elinor during the scene.
The family is present in the dining room at Norland Park.

memories.memory_text: He said my name—'Elinor'—so softly, so closely, I could feel my breath catch, and for a moment I thought he might finally speak the words I’ve longed to hear… but then he faltered, stammering about his education in Plymouth, about a Mr Pratt, as if none of it meant anything at all.
grounded_facts: Edward used Elinor's Christian name in a tender tone, creating a moment of emotional intensity.
Edward claimed he had something important to say but redirected the conversation to his education.
Edward mentioned being educated in Plymouth at a school run by Mr Pratt.
Elinor responded with brief, confused replies and felt increasingly bewildered.

memories.memory_text: I was counting our remaining money at the desk when Betsy remarked on the price of sugar—five shillings a pound—and I said, lightly, that we would have no more sugar then.
grounded_facts: Elinor was seated at a desk, counting money and making notes.
Betsy entered the room and mentioned the high price of sugar.
Elinor responded by deciding to stop buying sugar, stating it aloud in a light tone.

memories.scene_title: 70、INT. BARTON COTTAGE - ELINOR AND MARIANNE'S BEDROOM - DAY
memories.memory_text: I stood by the window, holding Edward’s handkerchief—his initials still faintly smelling of him—and pressed it to my eyes before the tears could fall.
grounded_facts: Elinor is in the bedroom at Barton Cottage.
She is looking out the window.
She holds Edward's handkerchief with the monogram 'ECF'.
Tears form in her eyes but do not fall.
She uses the handkerchief to suppress her tears.

memories.memory_text: I said aloud that they must have quarreled—Willoughby’s manner was too guilty to ignore, and when I asked why he left without word, Mama challenged me, as if suspecting the truth were a betrayal.
grounded_facts: Elinor states 'They must have quarreled' in the parlour after Marianne's outburst.
Elinor questions Willoughby's secretive behavior and suggests he left without assurance of return.
Mrs. Dashwood reacts defensively when Elinor voices suspicion about Willoughby.

memories.memory_text: I stood on the stairs with Marianne’s tea in my hands, watching Margaret run into the garden crying, and realized I was utterly alone—Mama shut her door on me, and no one would listen.
grounded_facts: Elinor takes the cup of tea from Margaret on the stairs.
Margaret runs into the garden in tears after being refused by Marianne.
Mrs. Dashwood shuts her bedroom door on Elinor, who remains alone on the stairs.
Elinor sits on the stairs drinking tea while hearing sobbing from multiple rooms.

memories.memory_text: I was turning back to the window when something outside made me stop—completely still—with the tea in my hand.
grounded_facts: Elinor was in the drawing room at Cleveland.
Charlotte was handing her a cup of tea.
Elinor turned toward the window and then froze upon seeing something outside.

memories.memory_text: I saw Colonel Brandon carrying Marianne in his arms, and for a moment it was as if Willoughby had returned—his posture, the way he held her, it struck me like a blow.
grounded_facts: Elinor sees Colonel Brandon approaching the house with Marianne cradled in his arms.
The sight evokes a strong association with Willoughby, described as being 'like seeing Willoughby's ghost.'

memories.memory_text: I took Marianne from Colonel Brandon, her body limp and cold, and helped carry her upstairs, knowing every moment mattered.
grounded_facts: Elinor physically took Marianne from Colonel Brandon upon his arrival.
Marianne was unresponsive due to cold and exhaustion.
Elinor assisted in moving Marianne upstairs immediately upon arrival at Cleveland.

memories.memory_text: I knocked on Mr. Palmer’s door in the middle of the night, my hand trembling slightly, and told him Marianne might need a doctor—my voice was steady, but my heart was not.
grounded_facts: Elinor was in her nightgown and approached Mr. Palmer's room at night.
She knocked on his door and spoke to him directly, expressing concern for Marianne's health.
She used the words: 'I think Marianne may need a doctor.'

memories.memory_text: I realized Marianne would be easier if her mother were here—only then did I understand how much I needed her too, though I could not say it.
grounded_facts: Elinor states that Marianne would be more at ease if her mother were present.
This moment marks Elinor’s recognition of the need for external support.

memories.memory_text: Colonel Brandon took my hand and kissed it—his quiet intensity made me feel both startled and strangely reassured as he promised to bring her.
grounded_facts: Colonel Brandon takes Elinor's hand and kisses it.
He offers to go to Barton to fetch Mrs. Dashwood and assures Elinor of her safety in his care.

memories.scene_title: 162、INT. CLEVELAND - ELINOR AND MARIANNE'S BEDROOM - LATER
memories.memory_text: I went to Marianne's bedside and said, 'Marianne, Marianne, please try—' in a practical tone at first.
grounded_facts: Elinor approaches Marianne's bedside.
She speaks to Marianne in a practical tone, urging her to try.

memories.scene_title: 162、INT. CLEVELAND - ELINOR AND MARIANNE'S BEDROOM - LATER
memories.memory_text: I couldn't hold it back any longer—my breath caught, I was sobbing without tears, clutching her hand and begging her not to leave me, whispering that I couldn't bear to be alone.
grounded_facts: Elinor breaks down with dry sobs, expressing inability to do without Marianne.
She explicitly pleads, 'do not leave me alone,' and kisses Marianne's hand repeatedly.

memories.scene_title: 164、INT. CLEVELAND - ELINOR AND MARIANNE'S BEDROOM - MORNING
memories.memory_text: I heard the faintest whisper—'Elinor?'—and turned with a cry, my heart lurching as Dr. Harris sprang up to examine her.
grounded_facts: Marianne whispered 'Elinor?' from the bed.
Elinor turned quickly in response and cried out.
Dr. Harris immediately stood and examined Marianne.

memories.scene_title: 164、INT. CLEVELAND - ELINOR AND MARIANNE'S BEDROOM - MORNING
memories.memory_text: The sound of carriage wheels on the gravel reached the window, and I whispered, 'My mother!'—suddenly not alone in this burden.
grounded_facts: A carriage approached, its wheels audible on the gravel outside.
Elinor recognized it as her mother's arrival and spoke her name in quiet urgency.

memories.scene_title: 167、INT. CLEVELAND - ELINOR AND MARIANNE'S BEDROOM - MORNING
memories.memory_text: I heard Marianne’s weak whisper—'Where is Elinor?'—and answered at once, my voice steady even as my heart clenched at the fear in her eyes.
grounded_facts: Marianne asked for Elinor upon regaining consciousness.
Elinor responded immediately with 'I am here, dearest, I am here.'
Marianne looked at Elinor with deep relief after seeing her.

memories.scene_title: 167、INT. CLEVELAND - ELINOR AND MARIANNE'S BEDROOM - MORNING
memories.memory_text: After she looked at me with such relief, I noticed Colonel Brandon standing silently at the door—his eyes wet—and then Marianne turned her gaze to him and whispered, 'Thank you,' so quietly it was almost lost.
grounded_facts: Colonel Brandon was present at the door, trying not to intrude.
Brandon wiped his eyes and turned away before being called by Marianne.
Marianne whispered 'Thank you' to Colonel Brandon after seeing him.

memories.memory_text: I was sewing in the parlour when Betsy mentioned Edward was married—just an offhand remark while leaving the room—and then everyone turned to look at me, silent, as if waiting for me to break.
grounded_facts: Elinor was sewing when the news of Edward's marriage was delivered by Betsy.
Betsy reported that Edward Ferrars was seen with Lucy Steele at the New London Inn, now married.
After Betsy's comment, there was a stunned silence and everyone in the room looked at Elinor.
Elinor attempted to ask a question but could not finish it.
Elinor eventually stood and walked out without further words.

memories.memory_text: I am very good at hiding—so good that even those who love me have failed to see what I carry.
grounded_facts: Elinor says 'I am very good at hiding' in response to her mother's admission of neglect.
The statement follows Mrs. Dashwood's realization that she has overlooked Elinor's needs.

memories.memory_text: There is a painful difference between expecting an unpleasant event and facing its final certainty—I felt it settle in me the moment I spoke it.
grounded_facts: Elinor says 'There is a painful difference between the expectation of an unpleasant event and its final certainty' during a quiet moment with her mother.
Mrs. Dashwood responds by squeezing Elinor's arm tightly, wordlessly acknowledging the weight of her words.

memories.memory_text: I heard Edward say that Lucy had married his brother Robert—that their engagement was broken, that he was free. When he finally said 'No,' I knew it was true: he was not married.
grounded_facts: Edward stated that Lucy Steele married his brother Robert Ferrars.
Edward confirmed he was not married when Elinor asked, 'Then you--are not married.'
Elinor rose and looked directly at Edward during this exchange.

memories.memory_text: I couldn't hold it back any longer—the weight of everything broke through, and I burst into tears right there in front of them all. I didn't even try to stop it.
grounded_facts: Elinor burst into tears immediately after Edward confirmed he was not married.
The emotional outburst shocked those present in the room.
Marianne, Margaret, and Mrs Dashwood left the room after the tears began.

memories.memory_text: I could not stop the tears, even as Edward spoke of being at liberty to love me at last—his words breaking through everything I had held back.
grounded_facts: Elinor is crying and unable to stop crying during the scene.
Edward declares that he is free to profess his love and that his heart has always been Elinor's.
Elinor perceives Edward's words as a release of her suppressed emotions.

memories.memory_text: I teased Edward, laughing at the absurd idea—'Me and Colonel Brandon!'—as if such a match could ever be more than a joke, though I felt a flicker of something warmer beneath the mockery.
grounded_facts: Elinor says 'Me and Colonel Brandon!' in response to Edward's confession about suspecting a romantic connection between her and Colonel Brandon.
The line is delivered with surprise and humor, indicating her reaction to an unexpected and implausible idea.

memories.memory_text: He stopped walking, stammered something—'Would you—can you—excuse me—'—and then took my face in his hands and kissed me, and I remember the warmth of his touch more than the words.
grounded_facts: Edward stops walking, looks at Elinor, and says 'Would you--can you--excuse me--' before taking her face in his hands and kissing her.
The kiss is a physical action initiated by Edward, marking a moment of emotional culmination.

constraints: Marianne must react emotionally before thinking practically
Marianne must express strong emotions through body language
Marianne should act on impulse when overwhelmed
Marianne cannot ignore someone she is emotionally attached to
Marianne must show physical signs of emotional distress
Marianne should avoid direct eye contact when self-conscious
dialogue_exemplars: Is love a fancy, or a feeling? No It is immortal as immaculate truth 'Tis not a blossom shed as soon as Youth Drops from the stem of life for it will grow In barren regions, where no waters flow Nor ray of promise cheats the pensive gloom--
Love is not love Which alters when it alteration finds Or bends with the remover to remove: 0, no! it is an ever-fixed mark That looks on tempests and is never shaken...
How like a winter hath my absence been from thee, the pleasure of the fleeting year! What freezings have I felt, what dark days seen! What old December's bareness everywhere!

My dear Madam--I am quite at a loss to discover in what point I could be so unfortunate as to offend you. My esteem for your family is very sincere but if I have given rise to a belief of more than I felt or meant to express, I shall reproach myself for not having been more guarded. My affections have long been engaged

but you--you I wronged above all. Only I knew your heart and its sorrows but even then I was never a grain more compassionate. I brought my illness upon myself--I wanted to destroy myself. And had I succeeded, what misery should I have caused you?
Esteem him! Like him! Use those insipid words again and I shall leave the room this instant!
Can he love her? Can the ardour of the soul really be satisfied with such polite, concealed affections?
It is too ridiculous! When is a man to be safe from such wit if age and infirmity do not protect him?
The dead are coming for the dying.
I suppose I have erred against decorum. I should have been dull and spiritless and talked only of the weather, or the state of the roads.

I shall mend my ways! I shall no longer worry others nor torture myself. I am determined to enter on a course of serious study---Colonel Brandon has promised me the run of his library and I shall read at least six hours a day. By the end of the year I expect to have improved my learning a very great deal.
Is there any felicity in the world superior to this?
But time alone does not determine intimacy. Seven years would be insufficient to make some people acquainted with each other and seven days are more than enough for others.
Always resignation and acceptance! Always prudence and honour and duty! Elinor, where is your heart?
But he shall have my unanswering devotion when you tell me he is to be my brother.

relations.relation_summary: Marianne and Willoughby share a deeply emotional and romantic relationship marked by intense mutual affection, intellectual connection, and gestures of courtship. Their bond is established through shared literary interests, particularly their collaborative recitation and discussion of Shakespeare's Sonnets, including Sonnet 116, which symbolizes their idealized view of love. This intellectual intimacy quickly evolves into romantic involvement, evidenced by Willoughby's affectionate gestures—giving Marianne wild flowers, carrying one of her flowers in his buttonhole, cutting and kissing a lock of her hair, and helping her into the curricle in a manner that draws admiration as 'the picture of happiness.' Their romantic connection is further confirmed by private conversations, whispered exchanges, and plans for the future, with explicit indications that Willoughby intended to propose marriage, a claim supported by Colonel Brandon’s assertion of Willoughby’s honorable intentions prior to financial complications. Marianne’s emotional state—her radiant joy upon seeing him, her feverish anticipation, and later her tearful distress over his departure and the examination of his personal mementos (sonnets, notes, lock of hair)—demonstrates the depth of her attachment and the relationship’s significance to her identity and emotional well-being. The relationship evolves from passionate courtship to heartbreak, as Willoughby abruptly leaves and later fails to acknowledge Marianne warmly upon their reunion, causing her visible pain. This shift marks their transition from lovers to former lovers, with lingering emotional consequences for Marianne, who struggles to reconcile her idealized memory of their romance with Willoughby’s cold detachment. The joint events they participate in—from driving off together in the curricle to their painful final encounter where Marianne attempts to greet him and he remains unresponsive—underscore both the intimacy they once shared and the tragic dissolution of their bond. The relationship profoundly impacts Marianne’s emotional arc, leading to disillusionment and personal growth, while revealing Willoughby’s internal conflict between affection and pragmatism.

The relationship between Marianne and Edward is characterized by Marianne's strong romantic interest and deep admiration for Edward's character, whom she views as exceptionally considerate and unselfish. This emotional affinity is demonstrated through her warm embrace upon seeing him and her vocal admiration despite others' skepticism. However, the dynamic is marked by emotional tension, particularly when Marianne confronts Edward about his prolonged absence, expressing frustration and emotional vulnerability. Edward, in turn, attempts to manage the situation non-verbally by trying to silence her with a glance, indicating discomfort or an awareness of unspoken complications. These interactions suggest a relationship where affection is present but strained by miscommunication or external constraints. The evidence is insufficient to confirm whether this relationship evolves beyond this point or is resolved, but the existing events highlight a one-sided emotional intensity from Marianne met with Edward's more restrained responses.

memories.memory_text: I saw Mamma take the letter about Barton Cottage and slip it into her gown without telling Elinor—I frowned because it felt wrong to hide something so important from her.
grounded_facts: Marianne witnessed Mrs. Dashwood hiding the letter in her gown
Marianne observed this action and reacted with a frown
The letter was about Barton Cottage and had not yet been shown to Elinor

memories.memory_text: I said love must be fire and passion—like Juliet or Heloise—because anything less, like Edward’s quiet ways, couldn’t possibly satisfy a heart truly alive.
grounded_facts: Marianne declared that true love must involve intense passion and sacrifice
She compared ideal love to literary figures Juliet, Guinevere, and Heloise
She questioned whether Edward could truly love Elinor due to his reserve

memories.memory_text: I was running down the hill, chasing the patch of blue sky through the mist, when I tripped and fell hard—my ankle twisted beneath me and the pain shot up my leg like fire.
grounded_facts: Marianne was running down a hill in the rain
She tripped and fell, spraining her ankle
She let out a sharp cry of pain and could not stand

memories.memory_text: I looked up and saw him—so striking, so sudden—and when he asked to check my ankle, I could only nod; then he lifted me, and I felt both dizzy with shame and trembling with something else I couldn't name.
grounded_facts: A stranger on a white horse appeared through the mist
He examined Marianne's ankle with her permission
He lifted her effortlessly, asking her to put her arm around his neck
Marianne felt overwhelmed by a mix of embarrassment and intense emotion

memories.memory_text: Our eyes locked the moment he set me down, and when he smiled at me, I felt it everywhere—my chest, my throat, like fire running through me—and I smiled back without meaning to, gloriously, as if we were the only ones in the room.
grounded_facts: Marianne and the stranger (Willoughby) looked straight into each other's eyes after he carried her in.
He smiled at her and she smiled back gloriously.

memories.memory_text: The second he left, I whispered 'His name! His name!' under my breath, urgent and desperate, because I couldn't bear not knowing who he was, not after that look.
grounded_facts: After the stranger left, Marianne hissed 'His name! His name!'
Mrs. Dashwood silenced her with a gesture.

memories.memory_text: I remember shouting his name like a prayer—Willoughby’s name—and how my heart surged when I spoke of his spirit and wit, as if saying it could make him real again. I didn’t care about my ankle, or the cold, or even Elinor’s mocking—only that he was coming back tomorrow.
grounded_facts: Marianne exclaims 'Mr John Willoughby of Allenham!' with excitement.
Marianne describes Willoughby with the words 'spirit and wit and feeling'.
Marianne declares 'And he is to come tomorrow!' with clear emotional investment.
Marianne ignores physical discomfort and social expectations, focusing entirely on Willoughby's return.

memories.memory_text: He pulled wildflowers from behind his back and bowed so dramatically—I couldn’t help but laugh. I remember saying how they weren’t from a hothouse, as if that proved they were real, like him.
grounded_facts: Willoughby presented Marianne with a bunch of wildflowers.
Marianne expressed appreciation for the wild, natural quality of the flowers.
Willoughby used humor and a courtly bow when giving the flowers.
Marianne reacted with delight to the gesture.

memories.memory_text: We both reached for Sonnet 116—'an ever-fixed mark'—and he had the same lines in his little book. For a moment, it felt like we weren’t two people, but one voice.
grounded_facts: Marianne declared Sonnet 116 as her favorite.
Willoughby recited lines from Sonnet 116 and continued with her.
Willoughby produced a miniature leatherbound copy of Shakespeare's sonnets.
Marianne reacted with delight and astonishment at the shared connection.

memories.memory_text: I said I don't regret showing my feelings for Willoughby. I believe seven days can be enough to know someone truly, and I feel I already know him well.
grounded_facts: Marianne expresses that she does not regret showing her feelings for Willoughby
She argues that time does not determine intimacy
She states that seven days can be enough to know someone
She feels she already knows Willoughby well

memories.memory_text: I accused Elinor of concealing her feelings, and the moment the words left my mouth, I saw her face—then she turned away. I didn’t mean to hurt her, but I can’t understand why she won’t let herself feel anything.
grounded_facts: Marianne speaks impulsively
comparing her emotional openness to Elinor's restraint
She immediately regrets her words upon seeing Elinor's reaction
Elinor accepts the apology quietly and withdraws into the house
Marianne expresses confusion and frustration over Elinor's emotional suppression

memories.memory_text: I looked up from my drawing and saw him there—Willoughby—his profile glowing behind the screen, so beautiful I couldn’t move, couldn’t breathe. Then I heard it, soft as a breath: *Marianne*.
grounded_facts: Marianne was drawing Willoughby's silhouette.
Willoughby was positioned behind a screen with his profile visible.
Marianne stopped drawing and gazed at Willoughby.
A whisper of 'Marianne' occurred during this moment.

memories.memory_text: He stepped forward, laughing, and asked if I’d finished—but it wasn’t the words that stayed with me. It was the way we looked at each other, silent, like nothing else was real.
grounded_facts: Willoughby moved out from behind the screen.
Willoughby spoke with laughter in his eyes.
Marianne and Willoughby exchanged a prolonged look after he stepped forward.

memories.memory_text: I felt the cold air on my neck when Willoughby cut off a piece of my hair, and then he kissed it—so softly—before tucking it into his pocketbook like it was something sacred.
grounded_facts: Willoughby cut off a lock of Marianne's hair while they were seated together in the window.
Willoughby kissed the lock of hair and placed it in his pocketbook.
The act occurred while Marianne was present and physically close to Willoughby, implying direct sensory experience.

memories.memory_text: I remember the wind rushing past my face as Willoughby drove us swiftly by the church, my heart lifting with every glance from the people watching—I felt radiant, truly seen, sitting beside him.
grounded_facts: Marianne was sitting beside Willoughby in his curricle.
The curricle passed by Barton Church while parishioners were present.
Marianne appeared as 'the picture of happiness' during the ride.

memories.scene_title: 77、EXT. BARTON COTTAGE - GARDEN PATH AND GATE - DUSK
memories.memory_text: He asked to speak with me alone tomorrow, and there was something so serious in his voice—it made me tremble. I know what this means: he is coming to propose.
grounded_facts: Willoughby requested a private interview with Marianne the next day.
His formal tone made Marianne feel shy.
Marianne agreed to stay behind from church to meet him.
Marianne believes Willoughby is coming to propose.

memories.memory_text: I couldn't bear their noise, their laughter—I just burst in, sobbing, and ran to my room, unable to face them, unable to speak.
grounded_facts: Marianne enters the cottage sobbing suddenly from the parlour.
She retreats into the room opposite without speaking.
She physically rejects comfort by shaking her head and waving others away.

memories.memory_text: I couldn't bear their questions—I had to get away, I ran upstairs and slammed my door, finally letting the tears come.
grounded_facts: Marianne physically breaks free from her mother's embrace.
She runs upstairs to her bedroom.
She slams the bedroom door.
She is heard sobbing in her room.

memories.scene_title: 89、INT. BARTON COTTAGE - ELINOR AND MARIANNE'S BEDROOM - DAY
memories.memory_text: I sat by the window, rain blurring the world outside, my tears falling onto Willoughby’s sonnets in my lap as I whispered his words back to myself—my voice breaking with every line.
grounded_facts: Marianne is sitting by the window in the bedroom at Barton Cottage
It is raining outside
Marianne has Willoughby's sonnets on her lap
Marianne is crying, with tear-swollen eyes

Marianne recites lines from a poem, specifically: 'How like a winter hath my absence been from thee, the pleasure of the fleeting year! What freezings have I felt, what dark days seen! What old December's bareness everywhere!'

memories.memory_text: I was pulling out ribbons and dresses, so full of joy—I kept thinking, I shall see Willoughby! I thanked Mrs. Jennings in my heart, I was that grateful.
grounded_facts: Marianne is excitedly preparing for a trip, handling ribbons and dresses.
She expresses deep gratitude toward Mrs. Jennings.
She announces her anticipation of seeing Willoughby with exuberance.

memories.memory_text: I looked at Elinor and she was so still, her back to me—when I asked about her whispering with Miss Steele, she said it was nothing, but I could tell something was wrong. I felt a flicker of doubt in the middle of my joy.
grounded_facts: Elinor is lying in bed with her back to Marianne.
Marianne notices Elinor's silence and strained demeanor.
Marianne questions Elinor about her conversation with Miss Steele.
Elinor responds that it was 'nothing of significance'.
Marianne observes Elinor's expression and feels uncertainty.

memories.scene_title: 98、INT. MRS JENNINGS'S HOUSE - DRAWING ROOM - DAY
memories.memory_text: I threw myself at Colonel Brandon, thinking he was Willoughby—I felt his coat under my hands before I even saw his face, and then I fled the room, humiliated.
grounded_facts: Marianne mistook Colonel Brandon for Willoughby upon hearing a man's voice and rushed to embrace him.
She immediately realized her mistake and said, 'Oh! Excuse me, Colonel--' before leaving the room hastily.
The action caused her visible distress and prompted her to flee.

memories.memory_text: I ran down the stairs so quickly, hoping—praying—there might be a message, but Pigeon just looked at me with that dry, unfeeling stare and said there was nothing.
grounded_facts: Marianne came running downstairs
Pigeon told Marianne there were no messages
Marianne appeared visibly dejected

memories.memory_text: Mrs Jennings took my hand and said the frost would bring them back to town soon. I brightened and said, 'Of course! I had not thought of that—thank you, Mrs Jennings!' Then I ran back upstairs.
grounded_facts: Mrs Jennings took Marianne's hand
Mrs Jennings mentioned the frost bringing sportsmen back to town
Marianne brightened
Marianne expressed surprise and gratitude
Marianne ran back upstairs

memories.memory_text: I remember slamming my pen down and snapping at Elinor not to ask me questions—my hands were shaking, and I couldn’t bear her quiet tone, as if she had the right to probe me while giving nothing of herself.
grounded_facts: Marianne is writing a letter when Elinor tries to speak to her.
Marianne says 'Do not ask me questions!' in response to Elinor's attempt to engage.
Marianne accuses Elinor of lacking openness, contrasting it with her own refusal to conceal feelings.
Marianne expresses emotional intensity through abrupt speech and implied physical agitation.

memories.memory_text: I read Willoughby's letter and said 'We are not engaged'—it was the first time I truly understood he never meant to marry me, though I had to defend him, saying he wasn't unworthy, that he had loved me even if he never declared it.
grounded_facts: Marianne reads Willoughby's letter aloud with deadly calm
She states 'We are not engaged' after reading the letter
She insists Willoughby did not break a vow because he never formally declared love
She defends Willoughby's character despite the rejection

memories.memory_text: After reading the letter, I begged Elinor to take me home—I cried out for Mamma and asked if we could leave tomorrow, even offering to hire a carriage, desperate to escape this house and the humiliation.
grounded_facts: Marianne cries out 'Oh, Mamma! I want Mamma!'
She urgently asks Elinor to take her home
She suggests hiring a carriage to leave
She expresses inability to endure staying at Mrs. Jennings's house

memories.memory_text: I sat on the bed, clutching Willoughby’s letter—so worn and damp from my tears—and kept reading it, as if the words might change, as if he might still come back to me.
grounded_facts: Marianne is sitting alone on the bed.
She is holding a creased and tear-stained letter from Willoughby.
She is repeatedly examining the letter.
Her notes to Willoughby, a lock of her hair, and pocket sonnets are scattered around her.

memories.memory_text: I just kept repeating 'But not enough. Not enough,' staring at nothing, feeling the weight of those words crush me.
grounded_facts: Marianne is sitting on the bed in a daze.
Elinor is kneeling beside her, holding her hands.
Elinor asks if she was right to tell Marianne the truth about Willoughby.
Marianne responds with 'But not enough. Not enough.'

memories.memory_text: I remember gasping when Mrs Jennings burst in, breathless, saying Edward had been engaged to Lucy Steele for five years—he chose her even after losing his inheritance.
grounded_facts: Mrs Jennings entered the bedroom abruptly and announced Edward Ferrars's secret engagement to Lucy Steele
Edward refused to break his promise to Lucy despite the threat of disinheritance
Marianne reacted with a visible gasp upon hearing the news

memories.memory_text: I whispered 'Oh, Elinor!' when I finally saw the pain in her face—she had carried this alone, while I never noticed, too lost in my own heartbreak.
grounded_facts: Marianne asked Elinor why she hadn't been told about Edward's engagement
Elinor revealed she had known since before going to London but was bound by Lucy's confidence
After Elinor expressed her suppressed suffering, Marianne whispered 'Oh, Elinor!' and then embraced her sobbing

memories.memory_text: I remember being carried in, numb and unable to speak, the world muffled and distant, my body too heavy to move, only aware of the Colonel's voice cutting through the cold, saying I wasn't hurt—*not hurt*—as if that explained why he was holding me.
grounded_facts: Marianne was brought into the house by Colonel Brandon
She was physically exhausted and unresponsive due to cold and fatigue
Colonel Brandon stated she was not hurt and needed warmth
Elinor and Mr. Palmer took her upstairs immediately

memories.scene_title: 167、INT. CLEVELAND - ELINOR AND MARIANNE'S BEDROOM - MORNING
memories.memory_text: I opened my eyes and saw Mama, but I couldn't turn my head—I had to whisper, had to know—where was Elinor?
grounded_facts: Marianne wakes up and sees her mother first.
She is too weak to move her head.
She asks in a whisper with urgent effort about Elinor's location.

memories.scene_title: 167、INT. CLEVELAND - ELINOR AND MARIANNE'S BEDROOM - MORNING
memories.memory_text: I saw him at the door, trying not to cry, turning away—I called his name, then just said, 'Thank you,' so quietly.
grounded_facts: Marianne sees Colonel Brandon at the door.
She notices him wiping his eyes and turning away.
She addresses him by title and whispers 'Thank you' after looking at him.

memories.memory_text: I stood on the downs and pointed to the spot where I fell the first time I saw Willoughby—my breath caught as I remembered how everything began with him there, and I finally understood that love alone wouldn’t have saved us from ruin.
grounded_facts: Marianne stops walking and indicates a spot on the ground where she fell and first saw Willoughby.
She reflects on Willoughby and questions whether marrying him would have led to happiness, considering his lack of money.
She acknowledges that emotional passion might not withstand practical hardships.

memories.memory_text: I told Elinor I wronged her above all—my voice broke because I knew I had seen her pain and still refused to be compassionate, and I could barely bear the thought of the misery I might have caused her if I hadn’t survived.
grounded_facts: Marianne explicitly apologizes to Elinor, stating she wronged her more than anyone else.
She admits she knew Elinor's heart and sorrows but lacked compassion.
She connects her illness to her emotional recklessness and expresses guilt over the potential pain she would have caused Elinor.

constraints: She must maintain decorum even when emotionally distressed.
She must speak politely to guests regardless of personal feelings.
She must suppress her own needs to support family appearances.
She must prioritize the emotional well-being of her children in her decisions.
She must express concern for others while suppressing her own distress.
She must communicate indirectly when delivering sensitive information.
dialogue_exemplars: To be reduced to the condition of visitor in my own home! It is not to be borne, Elinor!
John and Fanny will descend from London at any moment, followed no doubt by cartloads of relatives ready to turn us out of our rooms one by one do you expect me to be here to welcome them? Vultures!

'At last I have found a small enough
instrument to fir the parlour. I
expect to follow it in a day or two,
by which time I expect you to have
learned the enclosed. Your devoted
friend, Christopher Brandon.'

Hush, please, now that is enough,
Margaret. If you cannot think of
anything appropriate to say, you
will please restrict your remarks to
the weather.
Your father once told me not to allow you to neglect yourself. Now I find that it is I who have neglected you most.
Of course. But I hope she desires them to marry for love, first and foremost? I have always felt that, contrary to common wisdom, true affection is by far the most valuable dowry.
How very disappointing! But your business will not detain you from us for long, I hope?

He certainly is nor so dashing as Willoughby but he has a far more pleasing countenance. There was always
a something, if you remember, in
Willoughby's eyes at times which I
did not like.

Dear Mrs Dashwood, Miss Dashwood,
Miss Marianne and Captain Margaret--
it gives me great pleasure to restore
this atlas to its rightful owner.
Alas, business in London does not
permit me to accompany it, although
this is likely to hurt me far more
than it hurts you. For the present
my memories of your kindness must
For shame, Willoughby! Can you wait for an invitation from Barton Cottage?
Certainly not. I cannot force a confidence from Marianne and nor must you. We must trust her to confide in us in her own time.

relations.relation_summary: John Dashwood, a well-dressed and anxious man, travels urgently on horseback. John Dashwood actively carries out the urgent journey on horseback. John Dashwood is the one affected by the conditions and outcomes of the urgent journey.

Mr Dashwood refers to Marianne as one of 'the girls' and speaks of her romantic prospects, confirming she is his daughter. Marianne is referred to as one of 'the girls' in the context of Mrs Dashwood's family, and Mrs Dashwood speaks of her romantic future, confirming a maternal relationship. Mr Dashwood speaks about Elinor, Marianne, and Margaret while in the bedroom.

memories.scene_title: 3、INT. NORLAND PARK - MR DASHWOOD'S BEDROOM - NIGHT
memories.memory_text: Henry’s hand was so weak in mine, yet he clung to me as he spoke of the girls—Elinor needing a good husband, Marianne her poet, Margaret a pirate—and we smiled, though my heart was breaking.
grounded_facts: Mr Dashwood expressed concern about his daughters' futures while speaking to Mrs Dashwood. He mentioned Elinor
Marianne
and Margaret specifically with affectionate
wistful remarks. Mrs Dashwood concealed her fear and grief during this exchange. They shared a smile in response to their private humor about Margaret becoming a pirate.

memories.scene_title: 3、INT. NORLAND PARK - MR DASHWOOD'S BEDROOM - NIGHT
memories.memory_text: I passed John in the doorway, pressed his hand, but could not speak—my throat closed tight, and I let Thomas lead me out, knowing Henry was gone and that everything had changed.
grounded_facts: Mrs Dashwood left the room when John arrived. She passed John silently and only pressed his hand without speaking. She allowed Thomas to assist her out of the room. She was emotionally overwhelmed
suppressing speech and visible distress.

memories.scene_title: 7、INT. NORLAND PARK - MRS DASHWOOD'S BEDROOM - DAY
memories.memory_text: I was packing my things in such a hurry, the ribbons fluttering as I threw knick-knacks into the valise, and then I just collapsed into the chair—I couldn't bear the thought of facing John and Fanny in my own home, reduced to a visitor.
grounded_facts: Mrs Dashwood is actively packing personal items into a small valise.
She expresses distress at being reduced to a visitor in her own home.
She refers to John and Fanny's imminent arrival with dread, calling them and their expected relatives 'vultures'.
She suddenly collapses into a chair and bursts into tears.

memories.scene_title: 16、INT. NORLAND PARK - DINING ROOM - EARLY EVE
memories.memory_text: I said to John, 'My dear John, this is your home now,' and though the words felt like stones in my mouth, I kept my voice steady and my posture composed, as if I were merely passing on a truth long accepted.
grounded_facts: Mrs Dashwood speaks the line 'My dear John this is your home now' in the dining room at Norland Park.
The scene takes place during a tense family dinner with John, Fanny, Elinor, and Marianne present.
The atmosphere is stiff and silent, with visible resentment from Marianne and discomfort from Elinor.
Mrs Dashwood maintains a 'cool, removed dignity' during the exchange.

memories.memory_text: I smiled at Edward when he praised the stables so thoughtfully, and corrected Fanny so gently—his respect for us, even in small things, made me feel seen in a way I hadn’t since John and Fanny arrived.
grounded_facts: Mrs Dashwood smiled warmly at Edward.
Edward complimented the stables, not the lake view.
Edward corrected Fanny’s assumption about his room without defiance.
Mrs Dashwood perceived Edward’s respectful behavior toward her family.

memories.memory_text: I was sitting with the girls, sorting through yet more letters, each one too costly or too large, when Edward appeared at the door and said he had found what we were looking for—his words were simple, but the relief in Elinor’s face told me it was something real.
grounded_facts: Mrs Dashwood was present in the room with Elinor and Marianne, reviewing housing letters.
Edward entered hesitantly and announced he had found what they were looking for.
Mrs Dashwood was puzzled by Edward's elliptical manner, but noticed Elinor's immediate understanding and relieved reaction.

memories.scene_title: 27、INT. NORLAND PARK - DRAWING ROOM - ANOTHER DAY
memories.memory_text: I saw Edward teaching Margaret to sword-fight with such seriousness and kindness, no trace of mockery in him, just patience as she focused with all her might.
grounded_facts: Edward was teaching Margaret the first principles of sword-fighting.
Edward showed no condescension during the interaction.
Margaret was concentrating intensely during the lesson.
The event occurred in the drawing room at Norland Park, observed from nearby.

memories.scene_title: 28、INT. NORLAND PARK - VELVET ROOM - ANOTHER DAY
memories.memory_text: I was on the middle landing and saw Edward offer Elinor his handkerchief after she turned from the music, her eyes still wet. She took it with a grateful smile, and they began to walk out together.
grounded_facts: Mrs Dashwood was on the middle landing of the staircase.
She observed Edward and Elinor in the Velvet Room.
Edward offered Elinor his handkerchief after seeing she had been weeping.
Elinor accepted the handkerchief with a grateful smile.
Edward and Elinor began walking out of the room together.

memories.memory_text: I held the letter from Sir John Middleton in my hands and decided to hide it away, for I feared that moving to Barton Cottage too soon might tear Elinor from Edward, and with them so far apart, her heart would suffer.
grounded_facts: Mrs Dashwood receives a letter from Sir John Middleton offering Barton Cottage.
She considers Elinor's potential attachment to Edward before responding.
She chooses to delay accepting the offer and conceals the letter in her gown.

memories.memory_text: I announced our move to Barton Cottage, feeling the weight of every eye upon me, and though Edward looked heartbroken, I had to be firm—we could not outstay our welcome under John and Fanny’s roof.
grounded_facts: Mrs Dashwood announces the family's move to Barton Cottage.
Edward appears devastated by the news.
Fanny expresses false enthusiasm about the cottage.
Mrs Dashwood looks at Edward with compassion and then at Elinor.
She states they must leave soon to avoid trespassing on Fanny's goodwill.
She invites Edward to visit, ignoring Fanny's interruption.

memories.scene_title: 38、INT. THE LADIES' CARRIAGE - OPEN ROAD - RAIN - EVE
memories.memory_text: I looked at Elinor and felt a quiet satisfaction—Edward keeping his promise about the atlas meant more than he might know.
grounded_facts: Mrs Dashwood is present in the carriage with her daughters during a sombre journey.
Margaret mentions that Edward promised to bring the atlas to Barton.
Marianne expresses pleasure and makes a wager about Edward fulfilling the promise soon.
Mrs Dashwood looks at Elinor with satisfaction.

memories.memory_text: I remember trying to protest politely as Sir John and Mrs Jennings overwhelmed us with offers of game, fruit, and daily dinners, their voices talking over each other without pause—I felt so weary, yet I had to keep thanking them while managing my daughters’ silence.
grounded_facts: Mrs Dashwood spoke during the arrival scene, saying 'Oh, but dear Sir John, we cannot--'.
Sir John and Mrs Jennings spoke over each other, making the interaction overwhelming.
Offers of game, fruit, daily dinners, and carriage use were made repeatedly.
The arrival involved confusion, milling people, and luggage being carried in.
Mrs Dashwood was present in the group in the parlour at the end of the scene.

memories.memory_text: I remember standing by the window, the weight of Mrs Jennings’s words settling over me—'Not a beau for miles'—and then turning to see my girls bent over Margaret’s petticoat, their futures suddenly so uncertain.
grounded_facts: Mrs Dashwood is by the window in the parlour at Barton Cottage.
She recalls Mrs Jennings’s voice saying there is 'Not a beau for miles'.
She looks at her daughters mending Margaret's petticoat.
She feels anxious about her daughters' futures.

memories.memory_text: I felt my stomach tighten as Margaret blurted out about a man with no profession whose name begins with F, and I could only exchange helpless looks with Elinor and Marianne while Mrs Jennings and Sir John crowed with delight.
grounded_facts: Mrs Dashwood is present at a dinner with Sir John and Mrs Jennings.
Mrs Jennings persistently questions the Dashwood sisters about a potential romantic interest.
Margaret interrupts and reveals that the man has no profession and his name begins with F.
Mrs Dashwood reacts with dismay to Margaret's outburst.
Elinor, Marianne, and Mrs Dashwood share a moment of silent helplessness.
Sir John and Mrs Jennings respond with excitement and amusement.

memories.memory_text: I had to quiet Margaret when her words threatened to spill further into impropriety, reminding her that even if others speak freely, we must uphold our standards, especially before guests.
grounded_facts: Mrs Dashwood intervened during an argument between Marianne and Margaret in Sir John's carriage.
She directly addressed Margaret, telling her to stop speaking and to limit any future remarks to neutral topics like the weather.
The conversation involved sensitive subjects being discussed in the presence of others, which Mrs Dashwood deemed inappropriate.

memories.memory_text: I watched Marianne struggle with her bonnet, yanking at the ribbon in such a temper that it only grew tighter, and though I wanted to scold her for it, I couldn’t help but feel a flicker of amusement—until she cried out about never having peace, and my heart clenched at the weight behind her words.
grounded_facts: Marianne was removing her bonnet with visible frustration, making the knot tighter.
Mrs Dashwood observed Marianne’s outburst and felt both amusement and concern.
Marianne expressed distress over constant social intrusions, lamenting the lack of peace.

memories.memory_text: Elinor untangled Marianne's bonnet knot while I watched, and though we were all amused, I noticed her quiet patience. Later, I said to her, 'You must miss him, Elinor,' and saw her stillness, but did not ask more.
grounded_facts: Elinor helped Marianne with the bonnet knot while Mrs Dashwood observed.
Mrs Dashwood noted Elinor’s calm and patient manner during the incident.
Mrs Dashwood said to Elinor, 'You must miss him, Elinor,' implying awareness of Elinor's emotional experience.

memories.memory_text: I turned from the window, heart pounding, when I heard the commotion in the hall—Margaret burst in, soaked and breathless, saying Marianne had fallen and that Willoughby was carrying her.
grounded_facts: Mrs Dashwood was looking out the window, visibly worried, while waiting for Marianne.
She heard Margaret run into the dining room, saying 'She fell over! She fell down--and he's carrying her!'
Mrs Dashwood exclaimed 'At last!' upon hearing the noise in the hall.

memories.memory_text: I saw Marianne in the arms of a stranger, her face pale against the red of his coat, and the rain blurring everything—I could barely speak, only whisper her name.
grounded_facts: Mrs Dashwood sees Marianne being carried by a stranger up the garden path.
The stranger is wearing a scarlet coat, visible in the rainy setting.
Mrs Dashwood says 'Marianne!' upon seeing her daughter.

memories.memory_text: I asked Sir John where Allenham was, and when he said Willoughby is to inherit it from Lady Allen, I knew at once that he was a man of real consequence.
grounded_facts: Mrs Dashwood asked Sir John about the location of Allenham.
Sir John responded that Allenham is a small estate three miles east and that Willoughby is to inherit it from Lady Allen.
The narrative notes 'Now they are getting somewhere' after Mrs Dashwood's question, indicating its significance.

memories.memory_text: I greeted Mr Willoughby with outstretched arms, said it was a pleasure to see him, expressed our gratitude, and invited him to sit down. I smiled at Elinor as he sat on the footstool and began reading Shakespeare's sonnets with Marianne.
grounded_facts: Mrs Dashwood greets Willoughby with outstretched arms.
Mrs Dashwood says, 'Mr Willoughby! What a pleasure to see you again!'.
Mrs Dashwood says, 'Our gratitude, Mr Willoughby, is beyond expression—'.
Mrs Dashwood says, 'Pray sit down, Mr Willoughby.'
Mrs Dashwood smiles at Elinor with satisfaction as Willoughby sits on the footstool and reads Shakespeare's sonnets with Marianne.

memories.memory_text: I asked if they wanted us to starve—beef is already gone too, and all we have left is the barest comfort, but Elinor only speaks of tenpence and economy.
grounded_facts: Mrs Dashwood questioned whether the family was expected to starve when told beef was being rationed.
She reacted to Elinor's insistence on economizing by falling silent but sighing crossly.

memories.memory_text: I saw Willoughby lean close to Marianne, scissors in hand, and cut a lock of her hair—he kissed it before putting it in his pocket, and I said nothing, though my chest tightened at the sight.
grounded_facts: Mrs Dashwood witnessed Willoughby cutting a lock of Marianne's hair while they sat together in the window seat.
She observed him kiss the hair and place it in his pocket-book, remaining silent and showing only internal reaction.

memories.memory_text: I remember stepping down from the carriage, my limbs heavy with fatigue, and feeling Brandon's hand steady me as I struggled to stand—my only thought was reaching Marianne.
grounded_facts: Mrs Dashwood arrived by carriage at Cleveland in the morning.
She was physically weak due to exhaustion and emotional distress.
Brandon assisted her as she exited the carriage.

memories.memory_text: I asked Betsy if Thomas had returned from Exeter, and when she said he brought back two lovely fillets, I quickly explained it was because beef is cheaper there—and that they were for Marianne, as if that would make the indulgence justified.
grounded_facts: Mrs Dashwood inquired about Thomas's return from Exeter.
She received news that beef fillets had been purchased.
She offered a nervous justification for the expense, referencing both cost and Marianne's health.

memories.memory_text: When Betsy said Mr Ferrars was married, I asked her to fetch Thomas. When he came, I asked him who told him and whether Mr Ferrars was in the carriage with Miss Lucy Steele.
grounded_facts: Mrs Dashwood asked Betsy to fetch Thomas after hearing news of Mr Ferrars's marriage.
She questioned Thomas directly about the source of the information.
She confirmed from Thomas that Mr Ferrars was seen in a carriage with Miss Lucy Steele.

memories.memory_text: I stood among the villagers, clutching my bouquet, watching Marianne walk down the path in her white lawn dress—so radiant, so grown, her joy quiet but deep, and I felt both a swelling pride and a sudden ache as Brandon lifted her into the carriage like a man reborn.
grounded_facts: Mrs Dashwood was present at Marianne's wedding outside Barton Village Church.
She observed Marianne in a white lawn dress and Colonel Brandon in full uniform.
She witnessed Brandon lift Marianne into the open carriage adorned with bridal wreaths.
She saw the villagers throw flowers and celebrate as the couple departed.
She was positioned within the wedding party alongside Margaret, Thomas, John, and Fanny.
